# HYBE Executives Named 2026 Billboard Global Power Players

Six HYBE leaders recognized for global K-pop expansion under multi-genre strategy

By OSEN

Published 2026.03.10. 09:01

![](https://www.chosun.com/resizer/v2/HAYWKNBRMNTGCMTDHEYDSYJYG4.jpg?auth=64d638e947fe3f7cb5469db1f9523a00fe4e5993b53100beaa2a7b1fe806b83c&width=530&height=690&smart=true)

[OSEN=Seon Mi-kyung Reporter] Six HYBE executives, including Chairman Bang Si-hyuk, have been selected for the ‘2026 Billboard Global Power Players (2026 Billboard Global Power Players)’.

The U.S. music media Billboard announced the ‘2026 Billboard Global Power Players’ list on its official website on the 9th (local time). This list selects leaders who drive the growth and innovation of the music industry in global regions excluding the United States.

The HYBE executives listed include Chairman Bang Si-hyuk, CEO Lee Jae-sang, and Oh Yu-jin, head of HYBE Thirty-Sixty, who have been previously selected, as well as Isaac Lee, Chairman and CEO of HYBE America; Han Hyun-rok, CEO of HYBE Japan; and Kim Tae-ho, HYBE COO, who were newly selected this year. This result not only marks the largest number of HYBE executives selected in history but also broadly includes executives from overseas headquarters driving the ‘Multi-home, Multi-genre’ strategy, attesting to HYBE’s overwhelming achievements and cultural influence worldwide.

First, Chairman Bang Si-hyuk has been listed for the fifth time this year, following selections in 2018, 2019, 2021, and 2025. Chairman Bang has expanded the K-pop methodology into a universal formula for global pop success, achieving results in music markets across various regions. Notably, the global girl group KATSEYE, which he produced, entered the Billboard main chart for an extended period and was nominated in two categories at the 68th Annual GRAMMY Awards: ‘Best New Artist’ and ‘Best Pop Duo/Group Performance’, effectively sparking a cultural phenomenon. Additionally, in Latin America, he discovered and debuted the boy group SANTOS BRAVOS and the band Musza through reality programs and band auditions, respectively, expanding the K-pop system and influence to various parts of the globe.

CEO Lee Jae-sang, selected for the second time following last year’s selection, was recognized for strengthening the competitiveness of the music business, a core sector, and securing future growth engines based on platforms and technology, thereby solidifying HYBE’s position in the global market. In particular, he established HYBE China and HYBE India, driving the ‘Multi-home, Multi-genre’ strategy in the two largest and fastest-growing music markets in the world.

Oh Yu-jin, head of HYBE Thirty-Sixty, has been honored with three consecutive selections for overseeing global tours and festivals for HYBE Music Group APAC artists. According to Billboard’s recently released ‘2025 Boxscore Annual Report’, HYBE recorded $469.2 million in tour sales over the past year (from October 1, 2024, to September 30, 2025, hereinafter the same standard), ranking fourth in the ‘Top Promoters’ category and entering the top four in the global performance market.

The achievements of the executives newly selected for the list this year also stood out. Isaac Lee, Chairman and CEO of HYBE America, joined the list in recognition of his achievements in solidifying HYBE’s position in the Latin market. He co-produced the audition program ‘Pase a la Fama’ with Telemundo, which topped the Spanish-language broadcast ratings in Mexico during its time slot, and led the successful debuts of Musza, Low Clika, and Destino.

Han Hyun-rok, CEO of HYBE Japan, not only drove the overall music business in the Japanese market, including distribution of HYBE Music Group artists’ albums, concert promotions, IP projects, and marketing, but also successfully discovered and nurtured local Japanese artists. He contributed to the debut and growth of localized Japanese artists under the ‘Multi-home, Multi-genre’ strategy, including &TEAM’s first million-seller achievement and the rookie group aoen’s Oricon Daily Singles Ranking No. 1, thereby being listed for the first time.

Kim Tae-ho, HYBE COO, was also selected for the first time this year. COO Kim innovated the global fan experience by leading the convergence of entertainment and other industries. This includes the ‘THE CITY’ project, which expands fan experiences by providing various events and entertainment across cities alongside concerts, global pop-up stores, and the ‘Team Korea Cheer Sticks’ provided during the 2024 Paris Olympics, which spread a new cheering culture.

This year’s ‘2026 Billboard Global Power Players’ also included industry leaders such as Katsuya Kuroiwa, representative of avex, and Victoria Oclee, representative of the International Federation of the Phonographic Industry (IFPI), alongside HYBE executives. /seon@osen.co.kr

HYBE is a South Korean entertainment company originally founded by Bang Si Hyuk as Big Hit Entertainment in 2005.   
   
On March 10, 2021, it was announced that Big Hit Entertainment would rebrand as HYBE to better reflect its growth into a comprehensive content and lifestyle company. The rebrand was officially approved on March 30, 2021. As part of the restructuring, the original “Big Hit Entertainment” identity was retained as a separate music label under the new name BigHit Music, operating within HYBE’s multi-label system.   
   
BigHit Music remains the dedicated label managing artists such as BTS, TXT, and CORTIS. While these groups are part of the HYBE family, they are signed exclusively to BigHit Music, which functions as its own entity under HYBE.   
   
Their first group, BTS, debuted in June 2013, and their massive success helped propel BigHit—and later HYBE—into becoming one of the largest and most influential entertainment agencies in the world.

**K-pop* Hybe leaders named to Billboard’s 2026 Power 100 list*

# Hybe leaders named to Billboard’s 2026 Power 100 list

Published : Jan. 29, 2026 - 16:16:24
Updated : Jan. 30, 2026 - 18:13:34

Link copied!

![Lee Jung-joo](https://wimg.heraldcorp.com/svc/user/707a4c0d-5737-44db-a5ed-b059869a5240.jpg)

Covering K-pop issues,  
trends

![From left: chairman Bang Si-hyuk, CEO Lee Jae-sang and Hybe America chairman and CEO Isaac Lee (Hybe)](https://wimg.heraldcorp.com/news/cms/2026/01/30/news-p.v1.20260129.f5dcf41d7ae1482293d8d359026b1c81_P1.png)

Hybe’s top executives — chairman Bang Si-hyuk, CEO Lee Jae-sang and Hybe America chairman and CEO Isaac Lee — have been named among the most influential figures in the global music industry, according to Billboard’s 2026 Power 100 list released Wednesday.

Billboard annually selects key leaders across the global music ecosystem for its Power 100 list, recognizing those driving innovation across sectors such as labels, publishing, live performance and multi-sector leadership. The three leaders of Hybe were jointly listed at No. 30 on this year’s “Leaderboard” — a category spotlighting 40 global executives who are reshaping the music business.

Billboard praised Hybe’s “multihome, multigenre” strategy, highlighting how the company has successfully established international agencies while applying its K-pop-style artist and fandom development model to the global music market. It spotlighted Katesye — Hybe’s girl group co-developed with Geffen Records — as a prime example of that approach, noting the group’s Grammy nomination for Best New Artist.

Bang’s presence on the list marks his sixth appearance overall, having previously been selected in 2020 and consecutively from 2022 to 2025.

CEO Lee Jae-sang and Hybe America head Isaac Lee marked their first appearance on the Power 100 this year, recognized for leading Hybe’s global expansion as well as identifying future growth engines.

Lee Jae-sang, who spearheaded Hybe’s “Hybe 2.0” strategy, was credited for integrating content and technology to create new fan engagement experiences. CEO Lee was also acknowledged for playing a key role in the launch of Hybe China and Hybe India, accelerating the company’s multi-market strategy.

Billboard noted the strategic importance of establishing regional headquarters in the world’s two most populous countries, adding that Hybe artists Seventeen, Tomorrow X Together and Enhypen have all found success on the Billboard charts. According to Hybe, Seventeen topped the Top Album Sales chart with their fifth album, “Happy Burstday,” while Tomorrow X Together and Enhypen each reached No. 3 on the Billboard 200.

Hybe America CEO and chairman Isaac Lee was spotlighted for leading Hybe’s North American and Latin American expansion. Billboard noted that the company launched the boy band Santos Bravos through a Latin American reality series — a hybrid model combining K-pop production systems with Latin pop.

In addition to their appearance on the Leaderboard, the three Hybe executives also ranked fifth in the “Multisector” category, which evaluates influence across various parts of music-making and consumption — such as artist management and live entertainment.

Other executives named to this year’s Billboard Power 100 list include chairman and CEO Lucian Grainge of Universal Music Group, chairman Rob Stringer of Sony Music Group and president and CEO Michael Rapino of Live Nation.

# Concerts fuelled 18% growth for Hybe’s revenues last year

![Avatar photo](https://musically.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/08/cropped-stu-80x80.jpg)

### Share this:

![hybe logo](https://i0.wp.com/musically.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/07/hybe-logo.jpg?fit=780%2C260&ssl=1)

2025 was a strong year for the big hitters of the South Korean music industry. We reported on [SM Entertainment’s latest financial results](https://musically.com/2026/02/12/sm-entertainment-reached-new-q4-heights-with-16-6-growth/) yesterday, and now Hybe has [followed suit with its own record numbers](https://hybecorp.com/eng/news/news/9716?companyCode=&page=0).

The company posted annual revenues of KRW 2.65tn ($1.83bn at current exchange rates) in 2025, up 18% year-on-year.

The growth was fuelled by a big spike in concerts revenues, which were up 69% to KRW 763.9bn ($528.3m) thanks to tours and fan events from the likes of Seventeen, Tomorrow X Together, Enhypen and Jin out of BTS.

Recorded music? That was a different story, with Hybe’s revenues from that down 10.2% year-on-year to KRW 773bn ($534.5m). And yes, that does mean it nearly (but not quite) made more money from concerts than recordings in 2025.

On the positive side, Hybe said its artists generated around 3.7bn streams globally last year, while “accounting for around 3% of Spotify’s Global 200 chart share”.

The company also called out Hybe America’s success with Katseye, as well as its management partnership with South African artist Tyla.

Hybe says it’s already working on new projects for 2026 including “a new girl group debut in Korea, a successor global girl group in North America building on the Katseye model [and] a US-based boy group project in collaboration with Grammy-winning producer Ryan Tedder”.

However, the big news for Hybe in 2026 has already been announced: the return of its flagship group BTS. The company said it expects “a significant acceleration in revenue growth” this year on the back of that, and its other projects.

## How did Hybe transform from a Seoul startup into a global entertainment powerhouse?

Founded in 2005 as Big Hit Entertainment, Hybe pivoted from a small talent agency into a tech-driven lifestyle platform by 2021, acquiring a US management firm for over **1.05 billion USD** and expanding its ecosystem across music, tech, and retail.

![What is Brief History of Hybe Company?](//matrixbcg.com/cdn/shop/articles/hybecorp-brief-history_800x.png?v=1772966297)

Hybe grew from near-insolvency to a leader with market cap often between **8 trillion** and **10 trillion KRW** by early 2025, redefining artist–fan engagement through platformization.

What is Brief History of Hybe Company? It began as Bang Si-hyuk’s vision for healing music, scaled via artist success, strategic M&A, tech integration, and global partnerships; see [Hybe Porter's Five Forces Analysis](/products/hybecorp-bcg-matrix).

## What is the Hybe Founding Story?

Hybe originated as Big Hit Entertainment on February 1, 2005, founded by Bang Si-hyuk to prioritize artistic integrity and more authentic artist-fan storytelling. Early efforts focused on music production and songwriter management, producing ballad acts and soloists while navigating severe financial strain before the BTS era.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Star-Icon.svg)

### Founding Story

Bang Si-hyuk, known as Hitman Bang, founded Big Hit to fix what he saw as an emotion-light, factory-like idol system by emphasizing music, narrative, and artist welfare.

Bang assembled a small core team, including early executives like Lenzo Yoon, using music-theory expertise and digital trends to shift strategy; by 2013–2014 the company focused resources on the trainee project that became BTS, transforming Big Hit’s trajectory.

Financial context: Big Hit operated with limited capital through the late 2000s; following BTS’s breakout, the company reported revenue growth from under KRW **10 billion** annual scale pre-2013 to over KRW **800 billion** in consolidated revenue by 2020, reflecting the shift from survival-era bootstrapping to global commercial success.

Brand evolution: the name Big Hit embodied Bang’s ambition to create cultural impact; strategic rebranding and corporate restructuring later produced Hybe, marking a formal transition from a production-focused label to a diversified entertainment group focused on global IP, tech, and artist-centric models.

For operational and marketing context on the company’s strategic shifts, see [Marketing Strategy of Hybe](/blogs/marketing-strategy/hybecorp)

## Hybe SWOT Analysis

![Get Related Template](https://cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0911/3554/1625/files/hybecorp-swot-analysis.png)

## What Drove the Early Growth of Hybe?

The launch of BTS in June 2013 initiated a rapid Early Growth and Expansion phase for the company, driven by social media-led fan engagement and storytelling-focused IP. By 2015 and beyond, rising global sales and larger facilities reflected its shift from a small agency to an international entertainment platform.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Instead of relying on traditional TV, the company used Twitter and YouTube to build a global fanbase for BTS, a tactic central to the Hybe Company history and Hybe origins.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

The Most Beautiful Moment in Life series broke into international charts in 2015, proving the viability of storytelling-driven intellectual property and marking a key point in the Brief history of Hybe.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Growth required moving from a small Nonhyeon-dong office to larger Gangnam facilities to support world tours, merchandise, and complex digital content production tied to the Early history of Big Hit Entertainment.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Between 2017 and 2020 the company expanded beyond a single-artist agency, culminating in a 2020 KOSPI IPO that valued the firm at over **$4,000,000,000**, reshaping the Hybe Corporation timeline and founding of Hybe as a diversified entertainment holding.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

After the IPO the company acquired Source Music and Pledis Entertainment to broaden its artist roster, exemplifying major acquisitions by Hybe Corporation and the Hybe Company history.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

In 2021 the company completed a **$1,050,000,000** acquisition of Ithaca Holdings, integrating Scooter Braun’s roster and accelerating Hybe's global expansion history and transition from Big Hit to Hybe.

For analysis on Hybe’s market positioning and audience segments, see [Target Market of Hybe](/blogs/target-market/hybecorp)

## Hybe PESTLE Analysis

![Get Related Template](https://cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0911/3554/1625/files/hybecorp-pestle-analysis.png)

## What are the key Milestones in Hybe history?

Milestones, innovations and challenges in Hybe Company history trace the firm's shift from a music label to a technology-driven entertainment group, marked by platform-first products, global artist launches, and governance changes that reshaped its corporate trajectory.

| Year | Milestone |
| --- | --- |
| 2005 | Founding of Big Hit Entertainment by Bang Si-hyuk, establishing the origin of Hybe Company history. |
| 2013 | BTS rises to global prominence, anchoring the company’s rapid revenue and international expansion. |
| 2019 | Launch of Weverse platform, enabling direct fan engagement, commerce, and content distribution. |
| 2021 | Corporate restructuring and rebrand to Hybe, reflecting the transition from Big Hit to a diversified entertainment holding. |
| 2022 | NewJeans launched under the ADOR sub-label, achieving exceptional global streaming success. |
| 2024 | High-profile management dispute involving ADOR leadership triggers stock volatility and governance review. |

Weverse scaled to over **10 million** monthly active users by early 2025 and supported dozens of external artists, while Hybe secured multiple patents for digital concert and fan-interaction technologies. The platform-driven model generated nearly **1.2 trillion KRW** in indirect artist involvement revenue in recent years, cushioning the company during reduced touring.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Weverse Ecosystem

Integrated fan community, commerce and streaming platform that centralized revenue and analytics across artist rosters.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Digital Concert Patents

Patents for sync, multi-camera streaming, and interactive fan features that enabled monetized virtual events during touring pauses.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Multi-Label Structure

Creation of sub-labels (e.g., ADOR) to preserve creative independence while scaling management capabilities.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Global Distribution Partnerships

Strategic deals to expand streaming, publishing and merchandising reach in North America, Europe and Japan.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Data-Driven Fan Monetization

Use of user analytics on Weverse to optimize merchandise, ticketing and content release strategies.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Artist Diversification

Investment in new acts and acquisitions to reduce revenue concentration risk tied to flagship artists.

Mandatory military enlistment of key BTS members from late 2022 through 2025 compelled Hybe to pivot to solo projects and broaden its roster to sustain revenues. The 2024 legal and management dispute involving ADOR leadership highlighted governance weaknesses and prompted restructuring measures to stabilize operations.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Enlistment Impact

Mandatory military service reduced group touring capacity and shifted revenue toward digital channels and solo releases; Hybe responded by accelerating platform monetization and artist diversification.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Governance Crisis

The 2024 ADOR management dispute led to investor concern and temporary stock volatility, prompting reviews of the multi-label oversight framework.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Revenue Concentration

Heavy dependence on flagship acts exposed the company to artist-specific risks, driving strategic shifts toward platform revenues and new artist investments.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Regulatory & Market Risks

Global expansion brought compliance, licensing and market-adaptation challenges across territories such as the US, EU and Japan.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Balance of Independence

Maintaining creative autonomy for sub-labels while enforcing centralized strategy required governance refinement and clearer operational KPIs.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Financial Stabilization

Post-2022 shifts emphasized diversified revenue streams—digital, merchandising, publishing and new artist IP—to offset touring downturns; see [Revenue Streams & Business Model of Hybe](/blogs/how-it-works/hybecorp).

## Hybe Business Model Canvas

![Get Related Template](https://cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0911/3554/1625/files/hybecorp-business-model-canvas.png)

## What is the Timeline of Key Events for Hybe?

Timeline and Future Outlook traces Hybe Company history from Bang Si-hyuk's 2005 founding of Big Hit Entertainment through BTS-driven global expansion, strategic acquisitions, public listing and rebranding, to HYBE's 2026-facing HYBE 2.0 strategy integrating AI, Weverse expansion and US market localization.

| Year | Key Event |
| --- | --- |
| 2005 | Big Hit Entertainment is founded by Bang Si-hyuk, marking the start of the founding of Hybe. |
| 2013 | BTS debuts with 2 Cool 4 Skool, a pivotal moment in the early history of Big Hit Entertainment. |
| 2017 | BTS wins Top Social Artist at the Billboard Music Awards, signaling global dominance. |
| 2019 | Launch of Weverse, establishing a direct-to-fan platform and new revenue stream. |
| 2020 | Big Hit lists on KOSPI, raising capital for global expansion and acquisitions. |
| 2021 | Company rebrands to HYBE and acquires Ithaca Holdings for **1.05 billion USD**. |
| 2022 | BTS announces hiatus for solo projects and military service; NewJeans debuts under a multi-label model. |
| 2023 | HYBE ends its bid for SM Entertainment and becomes a strategic partner, advancing Hybe Corporation timeline. |
| 2024 | Jin and J-Hope complete military service, beginning phased returns to activity. |
| 2025 | Remaining BTS members expected to be discharged, setting stage for full-group reunion and tour. |

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Analysts project the full BTS reunion and global tour could push HYBE annual revenues toward **3 trillion KRW**, driven by concerts, merchandise and digital sales.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

HYBE plans to scale AI-driven music production via Supertone technologies to increase content output and lower production cycles.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Weverse is evolving into a broader digital lifestyle hub, aiming to boost ARPU through subscriptions, commerce and creator services.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

HYBE America is localizing the K-pop training and production model for Western talent to diversify revenue and global artist roster.

For a deeper look at strategic moves and corporate growth, see [Growth Strategy of Hybe](/blogs/growth-strategy/hybecorp)
